title: Sampling, labels and data quality

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Sampling, labels and data quality; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Data quality assessment examines provenance, collection process, consent/authority, sampling, missingness, duplicates, measurement error, label uncertainty, representation, time, and linkage to the deployment population.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A report groups missing labels by site and month, measures duplicate entities before splitting, samples label disagreements, and names populations absent from collection. Your goal is to produce a data-quality report and identify who or what is absent or mismeasured. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: python quality project; Problem and environment characteristics; Problem, target and success measure. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: Data quality assessment examines provenance, collection process, consent/authority, sampling, missingness, duplicates, measurement error, label uncertainty, representation, time, and linkage to the deployment population. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Cleaning cannot recover absent populations, missingness can be informative, majority labels can be wrong, deduplication can leak across splits, and a large dataset can still be systematically biased. The learner must reject this overclaim: Removing nulls and duplicates makes a dataset unbiased and representative, especially when the remaining row count is large.

includes: Inspect provenance, missingness, duplicates, measurement error, representation and label uncertainty.
Produce a data-quality report and identify who or what is absent or mismeasured.
excludes: Cleaning cannot recover absent populations, missingness can be informative, majority labels can be wrong, deduplication can leak across splits, and a large dataset can still be systematically biased.

outcomes: Produce a data-quality report and identify who or what is absent or mismeasured.

examples.title: Sampling, labels and data quality: bounded direct case
examples.situation: A report groups missing labels by site and month, measures duplicate entities before splitting, samples label disagreements, and names populations absent from collection.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: A report groups missing labels by site and month, measures duplicate entities before splitting, samples label disagreements, and names populations absent from collection.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Report provenance and measurement processes plus who or what is missing or mismeasured; cleaning changes data but cannot establish representativeness.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Report provenance and measurement processes plus who or what is missing or mismeasured; cleaning changes data but cannot establish representativeness.

examples.title: Sampling, labels and data quality: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: Dropping every incomplete row removes a subgroup with lower device connectivity, changing both representation and the decision target.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: Dropping every incomplete row removes a subgroup with lower device connectivity, changing both representation and the decision target.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Cleaning cannot recover absent populations, missingness can be informative, majority labels can be wrong, deduplication can leak across splits, and a large dataset can still be systematically biased.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: Removing nulls and duplicates makes a dataset unbiased and representative, especially when the remaining row count is large.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Produce a data-quality report and identify who or what is absent or mismeasured.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Removing nulls and duplicates makes a dataset unbiased and representative, especially when the remaining row count is large.
misconceptions.correction: Report provenance and measurement processes plus who or what is missing or mismeasured; cleaning changes data but cannot establish representativeness.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: Removing nulls and duplicates makes a dataset unbiased and representative, especially when the remaining row count is large.
